current_revision.content:
Emergent Ontology (/ɪˈmɜːrdʒənt ɒnˈtɒlədʒi/; abbreviated EO /ˌiːˈoʊ/) is a meta-theoretical framework built on a single claim: whatever can be expressed about transformation has tripartite structure. The underlying space — prior to any distinction, prior to any form — is khōra: undifferentiated, generative, undepleted by what it generates. As soon as any difference is discernible, three dimensions co-arise necessarily: ground, figure, and pattern, or abstractly −1, +1, √2 (the Structure triad's coordinates, used as default shorthand; see Triad-Specific Coordinate Systems for the full coordinate geometry). Applied at three independent levels (how something acts, where it acts, what it acts on), this generates a Capacity Ground: a structured possibility space whose geometry precedes any particular transformation occurring within it. That space is populated by distinct kinds of entities that interact differently depending on what they are, what they encounter, and where in the space the encounter occurs. The framework does not model particular changes but the expressible geometry of how anything becomes something else — and the ecology of what lives inside that geometry.

EO was developed by Michael T. Lacy beginning in 2024, drawing on investigative journalism, data systems architecture, and cross-disciplinary pattern analysis.

Origins

EO emerged from practical failures in institutional data systems rather than from theoretical ambition. As an investigative journalist covering Metro Nashville's arts funding, surveillance technology procurement, and homelessness services, Lacy repeatedly encountered the same structural problem: existing data ontologies — the classification systems that determine what a database can represent — could not capture phenomena in the process of becoming, frame shifts between competing interpretive lenses, or the systematic absences that shaped institutional behavior as powerfully as any presence.

A preceding project building a community intelligence platform for Nashville, involving multiple institutional partners and overlapping jurisdictions, crystallized the problem. Standard relational databases and classification taxonomies treated ontology as a storage question — what categories exist — rather than a transformation question — what changes are possible between categories. The gap was not a local software limitation. A 2023 workshop convened by the U.S. Department of Defense and MITRE Corporation independently concluded that the field needed "new data ontology models," having exhausted the explanatory power of SQL-based and classification-based approaches.

Core framework

The recursive distinction

EO rests on a single generative unit: the distinction between ground (what enables), figure (what emerges), and pattern (what coheres). This trichotomy — borrowed from Gestalt psychology but applied without requiring a perceiver — recurs at every scale of the framework and, EO claims, at every scale of the phenomena it describes. A thermal gradient has a ground state, figures that stand out against it, and patterns across those figures. So does a language. So does an institution. So does a database.

The recursion is not metaphorical. Applying the ground/figure/pattern distinction at three independent levels produces three axes, each with three values, yielding a 3 × 3 × 3 = 27-cell capacity ground. Empirical research using high-dimensional semantic embeddings has confirmed that the three axes are statistically independent (Adjusted Rand Index ≈ 0.05), meaning the same cognitive distinction applied at different levels generates genuinely new degrees of freedom rather than relabeling.

The three axes

Each axis asks a different question about a transformation, using the same trichotomy:

Axis

Question

Ground (−1)

Figure (+1)

Pattern (∨)

Mode (α / Identity)

How does it act?

Differentiating: withdraw, pull apart

Relating: bind, hold

Generating: produce, push outward

Domain (η / Space)

Where does it act?

Existence: whether things are

Significance: what things mean

Structure: how things connect

Object (Ω / Time)

What does it act on?

Ground: an ambient condition

Figure: a specific entity

Pattern: a regularity

Crossing Mode with Domain produces the nine operators (see below). Adding the Object axis extends the operator lattice into a 27-cell capacity ground.

The nine operators

The first two axes — Mode and Domain — cross to produce nine primitive transformation operators, arranged in a 3 × 3 lattice:

Differentiating (Ground)

Relating (Figure)

Generating (Pattern)

Existence

NUL (∅) — recognize absence

SEN (⊡) — register difference

INS (△) — create instance

Structure

SEG (|) — draw boundary

CON (⋈) — connect across boundary

SYN (∨) — merge into emergent whole

Significance

EVA (∿) — change value within frame

DEF (∥) — hold contradictions simultaneously

REC (↬) — change the frame itself

The operators group into three triads (rows), each addressing a different domain of reality:

  • Existence triad (NUL, SEN, INS): Whether things are. The ontological ground.

  • Structure triad (SEG, CON, SYN): How existing things relate. Organization and connection.

  • Significance triad (EVA, DEF, REC): What structured things signify — even in the most rudimentary sense of thermodynamic information, where states are distinguishable in ways that constrain what happens next.

The helix

The nine operators compose into a strict dependency ordering called the helix:

NUL → SEN → INS → SEG → CON → SYN → EVA → DEF → REC

This is a dependency map, not a checklist. Existence must be established before Structure can be organized; Structure must exist before Significance can operate on it. Within each triad, operators presuppose ground → figure → pattern: you cannot assign type (SEN) without first recognizing what is absent (NUL); you cannot connect across boundaries (CON) without first drawing them (SEG); you cannot change the frame (REC) without first encountering the contradictions (DEF) that reveal the frame's insufficiency.

The helix ordering was derived by exhaustive computational verification: of the 1,296 possible orderings of nine operators, 1,295 fail non-degeneracy criteria from Codd's relational algebra. Only one survives.

The 27-cell capacity ground

Adding the third axis — Object (whether the transformation targets a ground condition, a specific figure, or a pattern) — extends the 3 × 3 lattice into a 3 × 3 × 3 capacity ground of 27 cells, each specifying a unique combination of how, where, and what.

The capacity ground and the capacity ground are the same object viewed from different angles. The operator capacity ground asks: what transformation is happening? The capacity ground asks: what does it feel like to be at this coordinate?

Distribution asymmetry

The 27 cells are not evenly populated. A classification study of 1,192 English verbs revealed radical asymmetry:

  • The gravity well: One cell — Generating × Existence × Figure (bringing specific new things into existence: make, build, run, speak, write) — contains 399 verbs, a full third of the sample.

  • The desert: One cell — Generating × Structure × Ground (producing the background conditions from which structured wholes emerge) — contains zero verbs in English and is empty or nearly empty in 19 of 27 languages tested.

  • The lone occupant: Generating × Significance × Ground contains a single English verb: incubate — maintaining ambient conditions that enable fundamental transformation.

This distribution enacts the Gestalt figure-ground asymmetry the framework describes: language overwhelmingly foregrounds figures and backgrounds grounds.

The three faces

The capacity ground has three projective faces, each a 3 × 3 grid produced by collapsing one axis:

  • The Act face (Mode × Domain): produces the nine operators. Asks what transformation is happening. This is EO's most developed face — the one with an established dependency ordering.

  • The Site face (Domain × Object): produces nine terrain types (Void, Entity, Kind, Field, Link, Network, Atmosphere, Lens, Paradigm). Asks where in reality is the target — the phenomenological address before any operation is performed.

  • The Resolution face (Mode × Object): produces nine engagement stances (Clearing, Dissecting, Unraveling, Tending, Binding, Cultivating, Making, Composing, Forging). Asks at what grain is the transformation landing.

The analytical payoff lives in the interaction between faces. The operator alone tells you the what; the site tells you the where; the resolution tells you the how. All three together — captured in the three-face notation operator(Resolution, Site) — fully encode the capacity ground's three dimensions. DEF × Figure (deny, censor, suppress) is a different clinical situation from DEF × Ground (doubt, wonder, pretend): same operator, different site, different intervention.

Entity types

The capacity ground is not an empty coordinate system. EO identifies three kinds of entities that populate it, each a different configuration of ground, figure, and pattern:

Emanon (∞×)

Ground-dominant phenomena with ephemeral figures and shifting patterns. They exert definite influence in context but cannot be pinned down as stable entities. When examined directly, they multiply (∞×) — attempting to fix them as figures when they are ground effects creates phantom copies. Examples: team "chemistry," market "confidence," the "vibe" of a place, dark matter, the placebo effect.

Emanon are not bugs. They are structurally necessary features of any system complex enough to have ground effects. The more you try to eliminate them, the more they proliferate: each measurement creates new metrics, each definition spawns exceptions, each boundary generates edge cases.

Protogons

Pattern-dominant phenomena with forming figures and active ground. Identities are still crystallizing; transformation processes are underway; the space of possibility is opening. Protogons are unstable because patterns are seeking figure stability. Examples: forming relationships, developing theories, startups, emerging political movements.

Holons (∞↻)

Balanced configurations where figure, pattern, and ground reinforce each other. Clear identity, self-maintaining processes, organized context. Holons achieve recursive depth (∞↻) — they can apply the framework's own operations to themselves without collapse. Examples: living cells, mature ecosystems, established languages, stable institutions.

Dependency argument

The argument that the helix ordering NUL → SEN → INS → SEG → CON → SYN → EVA → DEF → REC is the unique non-degenerate sequencing proceeds in three stages:

  1. Operator mapping: Each of the nine operators is mapped to a precise equivalent in relational algebra and tuple relational calculus (e.g., NUL maps to Codd's NULL marker and three-valued logic; CON maps to the theta-join; REC maps to schema migration and recursive CTEs).

  2. Dependency proof: Intra-triad dependencies are proven within each axis (e.g., SEN requires NUL because type assignment presupposes the recognition of absence; CON requires SEG because joining across boundaries presupposes that boundaries exist). Inter-triad dependencies are established across axes (e.g., SEG requires INS because you cannot partition what has not been instantiated; EVA requires SYN because value change within a frame presupposes that the frame — an emergent structural whole — exists).

  3. Uniqueness by exhaustion: All 1,296 possible orderings of nine operators (constrained to maintain intra-triad sequence) are tested against non-degeneracy criteria formalized via functional dependency closure (Armstrong's axioms). 1,295 orderings fail. One survives.

The proof uses Codd's relational model as a mathematical witness — not claiming that EO is database theory, but that the dependency structure of the relational model independently instantiates EO's operator ordering, providing formal verification from an established mathematical framework.

A revised version of the argument (v2, February 2026) strengthened several elements identified in peer review, including formalizing Informational Content via FD-closure, rebuilding the REC-presupposes-SUP lemma around representational insufficiency rather than motivational appeal, and restructuring the SYN → EVA inter-triad handoff as a two-tier dependency.

Empirical evidence

Cross-linguistic findings

Beginning in February 2026, a computational study tested EO's structural claims across 27 languages spanning 11 language families and 2,500 years of attested usage, classifying over 32,000 verbs by operator and embedding their definitions in 3,072-dimensional semantic space.

Key findings:

  • Geometric reality: Verbs sharing an EO operator cluster together in embedding space far more tightly than chance predicts. In English (1,192 verbs), the separation z-score is −79 — seventy-nine standard deviations from chance. Significant clustering appears at the triad level in all 27 languages and at the operator level in 22 of 27.

  • Cross-linguistic universality: The structure is not an English artifact, not an Indo-European artifact, and not a modern artifact. Significant geometric separation was found in Korean, Classical Chinese, Arabic, Finnish, Basque, Sanskrit, Ancient Greek, Gothic, Old Church Slavonic, and Wolof, among others.

  • Orthogonality to existing taxonomies: ARI = 0.003 against WordNet's classification. EO captures transformation-type dimensions invisible to standard lexicographic categories.

  • Position primacy: How a verb transforms reality (differentiate, relate, generate) carves deeper geometric territory than what domain it operates on (existence, structure, significance). Positions produce stronger separation in 23 of 27 languages.

  • Universal SUP/REC poverty: The operators for holding contradictions (DEF) and changing frames (REC) are universally sparse, together comprising 0–5% of verb inventories in every language tested. This represents a species-wide cognitive limitation rather than a cultural bias — every human language is impoverished in the same place.

  • Typological variation along the position axis: Languages vary not in which operators they cover (all nine appear universally) but in their distributional strategies. Korean leans toward figure-position (SEN = 27%); Wolof leans toward ground-position (INS = 62%); Basque leans toward pattern-position (ALT = 25.6%). Grammatical typology predicts ontological preference better than cultural geography.

Biological grounding

Living systems provide an independent witness to the helix ordering. Reversals of adjacent operators produce not merely logical incoherence but biological impossibility: instantiation before designation corresponds to undifferentiated cell mass; connection before segmentation corresponds to signaling pathways forming before cell boundaries exist; superposition before alternation corresponds to seizure. Developmental biology independently instantiates the helix because embryogenesis is the helix: the sequential emergence of distinction (Existence), organization (Structure), and adaptive response (Significance) under selection pressure.

Convergent cultural evidence

Over forty independent cultural and philosophical traditions — spanning Vedic, Buddhist, Daoist, Indigenous Australian, Yoruba, Māori, Greek, Islamic, Jewish, and Christian intellectual histories — converge on triadic structures that map onto EO's operator architecture. This convergence was documented through a systematic crosswalk analysis rather than claimed by selective quotation.

The Experience Engine

EO's applied architecture for data systems takes the form of the Experience Engine, a formal specification for systems that process raw experience into interpretation through perspectival context. It is defined as a tuple:

𝓔 = (G, M, H, π, γ, σ)

where G is an append-only Given-Log (raw experience), M is a mutable Meant-Graph (interpretations), H is a Horizon-Lattice (perspectival contexts), and the functions map between them.

Nine rules govern the system, organized in three triads that mirror EO's operator structure:

Triad

Rules

Guarantee

Given-conformant (Experiential Integrity)

1. Distinction, 2. Impenetrability, 3. Ineliminability

Raw experience protected from fabrication and erasure

Horizon-conformant (Perspectival Coherence)

4. Perspectivality, 5. Restrictivity, 6. Coherence

Contextual access is structurally sound

Meant-conformant (Interpretive Accountability)

7. Groundedness, 8. Determinacy, 9. Defeasibility

Meaning is grounded, contextual, and revisable

Notable rules include the Anti-Gaslighting Axiom (Rule 3: raw experience cannot be erased or modified), the Anti-Omniscience Axiom (Rule 4: there is no God's-eye view; all memory availability is mediated by horizon), and the structural commitment to Defeasibility (Rule 9: no interpretation is globally immune to supersession). Every entry in the Given-Log is a **phenomenon** — an observation made by a specific agent, in a specific mode, within a specific frame — not a claim about observer-independent reality. The system records appearances, not things-in-themselves. Truth, in EO, is the limit that accumulating situated observations approach asymptotically: real, directional, never directly occupied. The deepest coordinate in the framework (2^√2, the Pattern position of the Significance triad) is transcendental by the Gelfond-Schneider theorem — reachable only in the limit, never by finite algebraic means. Rule 9's Defeasibility is not a methodological preference; it is a mathematical consequence of what truth is in a system where truth is a limit. Every entry in the Given-Log is a **phenomenon** — an observation made by a specific agent, in a specific mode, within a specific frame — not a claim about observer-independent reality. The system records appearances, not things-in-themselves. Truth, in EO, is the limit that accumulating situated observations approach asymptotically: real, directional, never directly occupied. The deepest coordinate in the framework (2^√2, the Pattern position of the Significance triad) is transcendental by the Gelfond-Schneider theorem — reachable only in the limit, never by finite algebraic means. Rule 9's Defeasibility is not a methodological preference; it is a mathematical consequence of what truth is in a system where truth is a limit.

Relationship to other frameworks

EO is situated in relation to several existing traditions:

  • Codd's relational model: Used as the formal mathematical witness for the proof, not as a claimed equivalence. EO recapitulates the foundational dependency chain of relational algebra while extending it into domains (contradiction-holding, frame change) that relational databases cannot natively represent.

  • Peirce's semiotics: The triadic structure (ground/figure/pattern mapping roughly onto Firstness/Secondness/Thirdness) shares deep structural parallels, though EO's formalization is independent.

  • Hegel's dialectic: EO's triadic structure is closer to Hegel's *Science of Logic* than the standard "thesis/antithesis/synthesis" gloss suggests — Hegel's categories are structural and mutually constitutive, not sequential. EO departs from Hegel on teleology (the dialectic has no destination; the helix spirals but does not arrive) and on closure (Hegel claims no remainder; EO names what it cannot hold).

  • Category theory: EO's operators can be understood as morphisms in a category where objects are database states, though the dependency argument uses relational algebra rather than categorical language.

  • Łukasiewicz's three-valued logic: Provides formal grounding for NUL's treatment of absence as an irreducible semantic primitive distinct from both presence and falsity.

References

  • Codd, E.F. (1970). "A Relational Model of Data for Large Shared Data Banks." Communications of the ACM, 13(6), 377–387.

  • Codd, E.F. (1979). "Extending the Database Relational Model to Capture More Meaning." ACM Transactions on Database Systems, 4(4), 397–434.

  • Armstrong, W.W. (1974). "Dependency Structures of Data Base Relationships." IFIP Congress, 580–583.

  • Lacy, M.T. & Claude (Anthropic). (2026). "A Relational Calculus Proof of Complete Operator Dependency in Emergent Ontology: The Unique Non-Degenerate Helix Ordering." Working paper, v2.

  • Lacy, M.T. (2026). "Experiential Ontology: Cross-Linguistic Empirical Findings." Research report, February 2026.

  • Date, C.J. & Darwen, H. (1995). The Third Manifesto. Addison-Wesley.
